md: Every polynomial has a deep connection between its **roots** (values of $x$ that make $y = 0$) and its **factored form**. For quadratics you saw it as $(x - r_1)(x - r_2)$. The same idea generalizes to any degree.

blocks.title: Factor Theorem
blocks.body: $r$ is a root of polynomial $P(x)$ if and only if $(x - r)$ is a factor of $P(x)$.

md: If you can find one root, you can divide $(x - r)$ out and reduce the polynomial's degree by one. Find all the roots, and you've factored the polynomial completely.
**Example.** $P(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$ has $x = 1$ as a root (check: $1 - 6 + 11 - 6 = 0$ ✓). So $(x - 1)$ is a factor:
The roots are $1, 2, 3$. Try setting $a_3 = 1, a_2 = -6, a_1 = 11, a_0 = -6$ in the widget below — you'll see the cubic crosses the x-axis at exactly those three points.
### How to find roots — the rational root theorem
If $P(x) = a_n x^n + \cdots + a_0$ has integer coefficients, then any *rational* root $\dfrac{p}{q}$ (in lowest terms) satisfies:
**Use this to make a candidate list**, then test each candidate by plugging in. The ones that give $0$ are roots.
Find all rational roots of $P(x) = x^3 - 4x^2 + x + 6$.

blocks.title: After finding one root, divide it out
blocks.body: If $P(x)$ has degree 5 and you find one root, divide by $(x - r)$ to get a degree-4 polynomial. Repeat. Each successful division reduces the problem until you can solve what's left with the quadratic formula or other techniques.

md: A root can appear *more than once*. $(x - 2)^3$ has $x = 2$ as a root with **multiplicity 3**. At that point, the curve doesn't just cross the x-axis — it flattens against it. Visually, multiplicity 1 = simple crossing; multiplicity 2 = touches and bounces back; multiplicity 3 = crosses but flattens like a chair.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Roots and factors are the same idea: $r$ is a root iff $(x - r)$ is a factor. To find roots: list rational candidates from $\pm \dfrac{p}{q}$ where $p \mid a_0$ and $q \mid a_n$, test each, divide out the ones that work, repeat. Multiplicity tells you how many times a root appears in the factored form.
